malay: Nasi Periuk Tanah (Ayam dan Sosej Wax)

food_subgroup: One-pot claypot rice with chicken, Chinese sausage, and salted fish

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI for Cantonese claypot rice. Closest parent: rice dish with chicken and preserved meats.
ontology.foodex2_description: Claypot rice Cantonese style (chicken, lap cheong, salted fish, white rice cooked in clay pot)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0RCK used as closest parent for mixed rice dish with preserved meats; no exact FoodEx2 code exists for this specific Cantonese preparation.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No specific SNOMED CT code for this dish.

serving.natural_language_portion: One claypot serving of Cantonese-style claypot rice, typically for one person, weighing approximately 450g.

nutrient_source.title: Claypot Rice — HPB Singapore Foodo + component calculation for lap cheong and salted fish
nutrient_source.organization: HPB Singapore Foodo (claypot rice entries) + USDA SR Legacy (chicken thigh) + Singapore brand labels for lap cheong + ICMR-NIN dried salted fish
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient data is primarily from HPB Singapore Foodo for claypot rice, supplemented with component calculations for specific ingredients: lap cheong from Singapore brand labels, salted fish from ICMR-NIN, and chicken thigh from USDA SR Legacy, to reflect the specific composition of this dish.

gi_source.title: Glycemic Index of White Rice and analogous cooked rice preparations
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (white rice base GI reference) and peer-reviewed literature on Maillard browning effects on GI
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI value of 72 is estimated based on the white rice base (GI ~72 per University of Sydney GI Database). The guoba (crispy rice crust) formed by Maillard browning contributes a higher local GI (78–82). Fats and proteins from chicken and lap cheong moderately attenuate the overall postprandial response. GL of 79.2 calculated from GI 72 × carbohydrate per serving (117g) / 100.

hypertension.key_factor: Extremely high sodium content (~2,610mg per pot)
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should consume claypot rice very sparingly; a single pot delivers approximately 2,610mg sodium — 130% of the WHO 2,000mg daily limit — primarily from salted fish, lap cheong, and dark soy sauce, which can significantly exacerbate elevated blood pressure.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Very high glycemic load (~79 per pot) and high carbohydrate content (~117g per pot)
type2_diabetes.guidance: For Type 2 Diabetes management, claypot rice should be consumed with extreme caution; a single pot carries a glycemic load of approximately 79 — equivalent to 2.5 standard servings of white rice — and can cause rapid and sustained postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sodium, saturated fat from lap cheong and chicken skin, and nitrites from processed meats
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Patients with cardiovascular disease should limit claypot rice intake; the combination of very high sodium (~2,610mg), saturated fat from lap cheong and chicken skin, and nitrites from processed meats collectively elevates cardiovascular risk and should be treated as an occasional indulgence only.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Extremely high sodium, nitrites from lap cheong, and high saturated fat presenting an unfavorable inflammatory profile
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: RA patients should treat claypot rice as a very occasional dish; the combination of very high sodium, nitrites from lap cheong (which may generate reactive nitrogen species worsening systemic inflammation), and high saturated fat from sausage is unfavorable — if consumed, removing lap cheong and salted fish and eating only a half portion is advisable.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Claypot Rice (Bao Zai Fan / 煲仔饭) is a traditional Cantonese one-pot dish where uncooked white rice is slow-cooked in a clay pot over direct charcoal or gas flame. The intense, sustained heat creates a prized crispy toasted rice crust at the bottom called 'guo ba' (锅巴), which is smoky and crunchy in texture. Toppings — typically marinated chicken thigh pieces, slices of Chinese waxed sausage (lap cheong), and salted fish — are layered on top of the rice as it cooks, steaming in the residual heat. At the table, a mixture of dark soy sauce and sesame oil is poured over the dish, sizzling on contact and infusing the rice with a rich, savory-sweet aroma. The dish is garnished with fresh scallions and cilantro. Nutritionally, a single pot (450g) provides approximately 890 kcal, 43g protein, 31g fat, and 117g carbohydrate. Sodium is extremely high at approximately 2,610mg per pot (130% of the WHO 2,000mg daily limit), driven by salted fish, lap cheong, and dark soy sauce. The glycemic index is high (~72) and the glycemic load is very high (~79 per pot), equivalent to consuming 2.5 standard servings of white rice in one meal. Salted fish is classified as an IARC Group 1 carcinogen with chronic high-frequency consumption (associated with nasopharyngeal carcinoma risk). This dish is a staple of Singapore hawker centres and Cantonese zi char restaurants.

confidence_reason: Nutrient data is primarily from HPB Singapore Foodo, an approved primary source for Singaporean dishes, with component calculations from other approved sources (USDA SR Legacy, ICMR-NIN) for specific ingredients. GI is estimated from the University of Sydney GI Database for the white rice base with documented adjustment rationale.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid fractionation data for this traditional mixed-ingredient claypot rice preparation are not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo, USDA SR Legacy composite entries for this dish, or ICMR-NIN as of the access date (2026-04-14); individual ingredient fatty acid profiles were not summed due to insufficient precision in ingredient weight estimates. water_g is set to -1: moisture content for this cooked mixed dish is not reported in HPB Singapore Foodo or the other approved sources consulted; water content varies substantially with cooking time and clay pot heat retention and cannot be reliably estimated. trans_fat_g is confirmed zero per HPB Foodo and USDA SR Legacy for the constituent ingredients. The food_subgroup in the input was truncated and has been corrected to a clean description.
